1: Standard revelation formula opening the allegory of Jerusalem. Chapter 16 is the longest chapter in Ezekiel and one of the most extended and intense allegories in the OT. The chapter uses the marriage metaphor that appears throughout the prophets (Hosea, Jeremiah, Second Isaiah) but takes it further than any other text — both in the detail of YHWH's grace and in the explicitness of Jerusalem's unfaithfulness.
2: ben-adam hoda et-Yerushalayim et-toavotiha: 'Son of man, make known to Jerusalem her abominations.' Toavot (abominations, detestable things) is the strongest Hebrew term for covenant violations, particularly idolatry and sexual immorality. The imperative hoda (make known, cause to know) places the prophet in the role of covenant prosecutor — he must formally charge the city. The form is that of a legal rib (lawsuit), which will culminate in the covenant-making and judgment sections below.
3: mekhorotaich umoladetaich me-eretz hakena'ani avich ha-emori ve'imech Hittit: 'Your origin and birth are from the land of Canaan; your father was an Amorite and your mother a Hittite.' The genealogical charge is startling in an Israelite context. Theologically the point is that Jerusalem's religious and ethical formation was thoroughly Canaanite — YHWH's grace did not find a worthy recipient but an utterly unsuitable one, making election even more scandalous. The contrast with the covenant formulas to follow (I gave you this and that) makes grace all the more extravagant.
4: beyom hilledet otach lo-krat shorech: 'On the day you were born, your navel cord was not cut.' The withholding of standard birth care (cutting the cord, washing, salt-rubbing, wrapping in cloths) symbolizes complete rejection and abandonment. Each item in the list represents a stage of neonatal care that loving parents provide. The absence of all of them communicates that no one owned or valued this infant. The social abandonment of Jerusalem at her “birth” is the baseline from which YHWH's subsequent care will be measured.
5: vatushlikhei el-penei hasadeh: 'You were cast out into the open field.' Infant exposure (hashlacha = throwing away) was practiced in the ancient Near East for unwanted or deformed children. The phrase bego'al naphshech (in loathing of your life/soul) captures the complete contempt with which the infant was treated. This image of utter destitution and rejection is the exact opposite of Israel's later self-understanding as YHWH's beloved and chosen.
6: va'omar lach bedamaich chayi: 'I said to you in your blood, Live!' This is the theological crux of the chapter. Bedamaich (in your blood) is emphatic and repeated twice for weight. The infant is lying in birth blood, abandoned, dying — and YHWH passes by and speaks one word: chayi (live!). The divine word is performative: YHWH does not rescue and then command, but commands and the command enacts the rescue. This is creation-power applied to election: life spoken into non-life. The double repetition (“I said to you in your blood, Live — yes, in your blood I said to you, Live”) is unique in the chapter and emphasizes the extremity of the grace.
7: revavah ketzemach hasadeh netaticha: 'I made you thrive like a plant of the field.' Revavah (ten thousand, myriad) conveys abundant, explosive growth. The language shifts from infant to mature woman — breasts developed, hair grew — language of physical maturation marking the transition from childhood to marriageable age. Yet the phrase ve'at eriyah ve'eryah (but you were naked and bare) closes the verse with vulnerability: even at full physical development, she had no covering — no social status, no covenant protection.
8: va'ephros kenaph alayich va'akasse ervatach: 'I spread my corner over you and covered your nakedness.' Kanaph (wing, corner of garment) is the technical term for the marriage gesture: in Ruth 3:9 Ruth asks Boaz to spread his kanaph over her as a kinsman-redeemer. Here YHWH performs the same act — he claims Jerusalem as his wife by the covenant (va'avo veshevuat = I entered into a covenant/oath with you). The covenant formula follows: vatihyi li (you became mine). Divine election is cast as covenant marriage — the most intimate relational category available.
9: va'erchatzech bemayim: 'I washed you with water.' The washing that was withheld at birth (Ezekiel 16:4) YHWH now performs as a marriage preparation. The sequence (wash, anoint with oil, clothe) mirrors the preparation of a bride. Shemen (oil) for anointing was both cosmetic and symbolic of blessing and dignity. The reversal is total: the unbathed, unanointed, naked infant is now bathed, anointed, and adorned as a wife.
10: va'albishech riqmah: 'I clothed you in embroidery.' The clothing list escalates: riqmah (embroidered cloth), tahhash (fine leather, possibly dugong or dolphin skin — the same material used for the tabernacle coverings in Exodus 26:14), shesh (fine linen/byssus), meshiy (silk or fine cloth). The pairing with tabernacle materials (tahhash) subtly links YHWH's adornment of Jerusalem to the construction of the sanctuary — the divine dwelling and the chosen city adorned with the same precious materials.
11: va'ede otach edi: 'I adorned you with adornments.' The jewelry list — bracelets, necklace, nose ring, earrings, crown — represents royal bridal adornment. Each item is a gift, transforming the abandoned infant into a queen. The accumulation of gifts communicates the extravagance of divine grace: not merely life but abundant flourishing, not merely covering but royal dignity.
12: va'eten netzer tiferet beroshech: 'I put a beautiful crown on your head.' Atara (crown) completes the royal status conferred. By Ezekiel 16:13 Jerusalem has become a queen (vatitzlehi limluchah, you prospered into royalty/queenship). The entire adornment sequence is a deliberate reversal of the birth scene: every deprivation of Ezekiel 16:4-5 is superseded. The divine generosity is not partial restoration but complete transformation.
13: vatiphe me'od me'od vatitzlehi limluchah: 'You became exceedingly beautiful and prospered into royalty.' Tiphah (beauty) and tzlichah limluchah (prospered into queenship/royalty) mark the apex of the divine gift. The beauty is explicitly YHWH's own gift: yophyech is qualified by asher samti alayich (which I had placed upon you). This sets up the tragic irony of Ezekiel 16:15 — she trusts in a beauty that was never hers but only YHWH's gift through her.
14: vateze lecha shem bagoyim beyopheych: 'Your fame went out among the nations because of your beauty.' Shem (name, fame, reputation) going out among the nations recalls the reputation of Solomon's Jerusalem (1 Kgs 10), which was at its peak of beauty and wisdom. The beauty was kalil (complete, perfect) and behodi (in my [YHWH's] splendor/majesty) — language of theophanic glory applied to the city. YHWH's glory was meant to shine through Jerusalem to the nations; her subsequent defection is therefore not just a personal betrayal but a desecration of YHWH's glory-display.
15: vativti al-yiphyech vatizni al-shemech: 'But you trusted in your beauty and played the harlot because of your fame.' Vatavti (you trusted) is the decisive theological verb: batach (trust, rely on) in the OT is rightly directed to YHWH alone (Psalms 115:9-11); misplaced trust is the root of idolatry and apostasy. She trusted in beauty that was YHWH's gift — making a gift into a god. Vatizni (you played the harlot) is the prophetic metaphor for idolatry that Hosea pioneered; here it encompasses both literal cult prostitution and the political alliances that treated the nations as Israel's true security rather than YHWH.
16: vatikchi mibigdei-otach vatasi lach bamot tilaot: 'You took from your garments and made for yourself colorful high places.' The misuse of YHWH's gifts (garments → shrines; jewelry → idols in Ezekiel 16:17-18) is the structural logic of the indictment: the gifts of grace are the raw material for apostasy. Bamot (high places) were the localized shrines where illicit worship was offered. The phrase lo bau velo yihyeh (such things have not come and shall not be) is a condemnation formula indicating the unique monstrousness of the act.
17: vatikchi kli tiph'artecha mizahavi umikaspi: 'You took your beautiful jewels of gold and silver, which I had given you, and made male images.' Kli tiphartecha (articles of your splendor/beauty) — the same gifts from Ezekiel 16:11-12 now repurposed. Tzalmei zachar (male images/figures) likely refers to phallic cult images associated with fertility worship. The irony is stark: YHWH the divine husband's gifts are fashioned into images that substitute for him as the object of desire and worship.
18: vatilbeshi otam riqmatecha: 'You clothed them with your embroidered garments.' The adorning of idols with YHWH's gifts of clothing and food (Ezekiel 16:18-19) represents a total inversion: the things YHWH gave to adorn his bride are given to clothe the idols. Ve'qitorati ve'shemeni (my incense and my oil) — YHWH calls the offerings “mine” because they were his gifts first. The idol feast is made from YHWH's provisions, a culpable misappropriation.
19: velachemit asher natati lach: 'And my food which I gave you.' The catalogue of sacrificial food given to idols (fine flour, oil, honey) emphasizes that YHWH himself provided the materials for the apostasy. There is something profoundly offensive about using the benefactor's gifts to honor a rival — it is the behavior of a spouse using a wedding gift to buy presents for a lover.
20: vatikchi et-baniach ve'et-benotaich asher yaladti li: 'And you took your sons and daughters whom you bore to me, and you sacrificed them to be devoured.' This verse marks the climactic intensification of the indictment. Asher yaladti li (whom you bore to me) — YHWH claims the children as his own (they are the fruit of the divine-human covenant union). Child sacrifice (zivach lahem le'echol) is the ultimate desecration: covenant offspring given to the fire of Molech (cf. 2 Kings 23:10; Jeremiah 7:31). Ezekiel returns to this charge repeatedly (Ezekiel 20:25-26, 31) because it represents the covenant's complete inversion: the children given by YHWH's grace destroyed in service of foreign gods.
21: vatishchati et-banai vatitnenem beha'avir otam lahem: 'You slaughtered my children and delivered them up, passing them through the fire for them.' The repetition of the child sacrifice charge (Ezekiel 16:20 → Ezekiel 16:21) emphasizes horror through redundancy. Vatishchati (you slaughtered) uses the technical slaughter term, treating the children as sacrificial animals. Beha'avir otam (passing them through) is the technical phrase for the Molech rite (cf. Leviticus 18:21; Deuteronomy 18:10). The rhetorical question implied — could this have been in addition to all your other abominations? — sets up the litany of unfaithfulness still to come.
22: ve'et kol-toavotaich uzinutaich lo zakart et-yemei neurayich: 'In all your abominations and prostitutions you did not remember the days of your youth.' Lo zakart (you did not remember) is the covenant's antithetical response to YHWH's own remembering (he will “remember the covenant” in Ezekiel 16:60). Memory in covenant language means more than mental recall — it means active acknowledgment with the appropriate response. Forgetting the days of YHWH's tender care (the naked infant, the life-giving word, the bridal adornment) is the structural apostasy underlying all the specific acts of unfaithfulness.
23: vayehi acharei kol-raateki oy oy lach: 'After all your evil — woe, woe to you!' The double oy (woe, alas) is an exclamation of profound grief and horror — the prophet's own voice breaking through the divine speech. The exclamation interrupts the catalogue of sin to express the lament that both YHWH and the prophet feel at the magnitude of Jerusalem's unfaithfulness.
24: vatibni lach gav: 'You built yourself a platform/mound.' Gav (mound, platform) and ramah (high place) in Ezekiel 16:25 refer to the elevated shrines used for illicit worship. That Jerusalem built these structures throughout the city (“at every street corner” in Ezekiel 16:25) indicates the normalization and omnipresence of the cult prostitution metaphor — unfaithfulness was not occasional but systematic and public.
25: el kol-rosh derech banit ramatayich: 'At the head of every road you built your high places.' The public exposure of the prostitution — at the intersection of every major road — mirrors the publicity of Israel's covenant-breaking. Vatephtchi et-raglayich (you spread your legs) is the most explicit sexual language in the Hebrew Bible, used here not for pornographic effect but to capture the brazenness and public nature of Israel's apostasy. The phrase vatarbiy et-taznutaich (you multiplied your prostitutions) quantifies the extent of the unfaithfulness.
26: vatizni el-benei Mitzraim: 'You played the harlot with the sons of Egypt.' The first geopolitical alliance named as “prostitution” is Egypt — Israel's first and foundational political temptation. Egypt represents not just an ally but a parent-figure (Israel came from Egypt), making the political alliance a kind of return to slavery. Gedolei vasar (great of flesh) is anatomical language indicating the power/virility of Egypt as a military force. The attraction is size and power — the opposite of YHWH's way of choosing the weak to shame the strong (cf. Deuteronomy 7:7-8).
27: ve'hinei natati et-yadi alayich: 'Behold I stretched out my hand against you.' The divine hand (yad) stretched out in judgment is the Exodus formula inverted — YHWH's hand that delivered from Egypt (Exodus 3:20; Ezekiel 6:6) now acts in judgment against Israel. YHWH diminished her food portion (va'agra chukateich) as a preliminary corrective judgment. That even the daughter of the Philistines was ashamed of Jerusalem's behavior measures how far she had fallen — nations without covenant shame Israel that has covenant.
28: vatizni el-benei Ashshur: 'You played the harlot with the sons of Assyria.' Assyria represents the next political temptation — the imperial power to the east that dominated the 8th century BCE. The iterative structure of the indictment (Egypt → Assyria → Babylonia) maps onto the historical sequence of Israel's political alliances. The phrase velo savat (you were not satisfied) captures the insatiability of idolatrous desire — each new alliance promises security that it cannot deliver, yet the appetite for the next grows.
29: vatarbiy et-taznutech el-eretz kena'an Kasdiymah: 'You multiplied your prostitutions to the land of the traders, Chaldea.' Kasdimah (Chaldea/Babylon) is the third and greatest of the alliance-prostitutions. The velo savat gam-zot (you were not satisfied with this either) is the final measure of insatiability. By listing Egypt, Assyria, and Babylon, Ezekiel has indicted every major empire of his day — Jerusalem had prostituted herself to all of them in turn, finding none of them able to satisfy the longing that only YHWH can fill.
30: mah amulah libatach: 'How sick is your heart!' or 'How enflamed your heart!' Amulah is a hapax legomenon (appearing only here) meaning weak/sick or intensely desirous; LXX rendered it as “enflamed.” The lev (heart) is the seat of decision and desire in Hebrew psychology. The adjective captures the disordered desire that lies behind all the acts of apostasy: a heart that is insatiably inflamed toward what cannot satisfy it.
31: binhot gavech bekhol-rosh derech: 'In building your platform at every street corner.' The detailed geography of the illicit shrines — at road junctions, in every square — conveys the total saturation of the city with idolatry. The climactic inversion: she was not like a harlot (zah) who receives payment — she was the opposite, giving payment to her lovers. This reversal is both economically and theologically significant: ordinary prostitution is at least transactional; Jerusalem's apostasy is gratuitous, giving away YHWH's resources to pursue empty substitutes.
32: ha'ishah hameno'efet: 'The adulterous woman.' The shift from prostitution metaphor to adultery metaphor (ni'uf = adultery) tightens the covenantal charge: adultery requires the prior bond of marriage. Prostitution with strangers may be reprehensible but adultery is specifically a covenant violation. By calling Jerusalem ha-ishah hameno'efet (the adulterous woman), YHWH identifies the real offense — it is not merely immorality but covenant-breaking against the divine husband.
33: el-kol-zonot yitenu nadan: 'To all prostitutes gifts are given.' The economic reversal is devastating: every prostitute receives payment for her services, but Jerusalem paid her lovers. Nadan (gift, bride-price, payment) is here the payment given to a prostitute. The observation that Jerusalem inverted the economic logic of prostitution compounds her shame — she did not even have the dignity of being a paid prostitute, but gave YHWH's gifts away to attract alliances that profited her nothing.
34: vatehiy bach hapech min-hanashim bitznutaich: 'So you were the opposite among women in your prostitution.' The summary of the economic reversal seals the indictment: she paid rather than was paid, so she was the hapech (reverse, opposite) of the normal prostitute. This is a legal summary before the sentencing. The peculiar monstrousness of Jerusalem's behavior is that she was not merely unfaithful but irrationally, wastefully unfaithful — spending covenant resources to pursue worthless substitutes.
35: lachem zonah shim'i devar YHWH: 'Therefore, O prostitute, hear the word of YHWH.' The judicial summons to hear (shim'i) initiates the sentence phase. The direct address as zonah (prostitute) is the formal judicial charge: you are convicted of what has been described. The transition from indictment (Ezekiel 16:2-34) to sentencing (Ezekiel 16:35-43) follows the pattern of the prophetic rib (covenant lawsuit).
36: ya'an hishafech nechushtech: 'Because your bronze/nakedness was poured out.' Nechoshtet is debated: LXX read it as “your bronze” (offering bowls?), but the context suggests it is a euphemism for the exposure of nakedness (erva). The sentence matches the crime: she exposed herself publicly in her prostitution, so she will be publicly exposed in judgment. This lex talionis logic (punishment mirrors offense) is characteristic of Ezekiel's jurisprudence.
37: lachem hineni mekabetz et-kol-me'ahavayich: 'Therefore I will gather all your lovers.' The judgment is that YHWH will convene the very nations she pursued and turn them against her. The lovers (Egypt, Assyria, Babylon) become the instruments of judgment — the objects of her misplaced trust become the agents of her destruction. This is the characteristic prophetic irony: the idols one trusts become the instruments of YHWH's punishment.
38: ushphattich mishpetei no'afot veshofechot dam: 'I will judge you as women who commit adultery and murder are judged.' The dual charge is adultery (covenant-breaking with YHWH) and murder (child sacrifice). Both capital offenses under Torah (Leviticus 20:10; Ezekiel 20:2). The judgment formulation ve'netaticha dam cheima vekina (I will make you blood of wrath and jealousy) invokes the divine jealousy (qin'ah) that is YHWH's response to covenant violation — his covenant love turned to wrath by infidelity.
39: venatati otach beyadam venatezu gavech: 'I will give you into their hands, and they shall tear down your platform.' The stripping and abandonment by the lovers enacts the exact reversal of Ezekiel 16:10-14: the clothing, jewels, and structures given by YHWH that she gave to idols will be stripped from her. The lovers who received payment will take everything — another ironic reversal of the established economic logic.
40: vehe'elu alayich kahal vesaqelu otach be'even: 'They shall bring up a congregation against you and stone you.' Stoning was the Torah penalty for adultery (Deuteronomy 22:24) and for the daughter of a priest who became a prostitute (Leviticus 21:9). The assembly convened to execute judgment mirrors the assembly of lovers she gathered. Kahal (assembly, congregation) is the same word used for the assembly at Sinai — the covenant assembly becomes the judicial assembly executing the sentence for covenant violation.
41: vesarfu vateicha ba'esh: 'They shall burn your houses with fire.' The burning of the houses completes the stripping — buildings as well as clothing, jewels, and idols are destroyed. The phrase ve'asiti vach shefatim le'einei nashim rabbot (I will execute judgments on you in the sight of many women) indicates that the sentence will be public — a deterrent to other nations who might follow Jerusalem's example of covenant violation.
42: vahishbati chamatי uminaich: 'I will satisfy my wrath against you, and my jealousy will depart from you.' The exhaustion of divine hemat (wrath/burning anger) and qin'ah (jealousy/zeal) signals that the judgment, though terrible, is bounded: it is not permanent annihilation but a judicial sentence that has a conclusion. The phrase ve'shaqatty (I will be quiet/calm) indicates YHWH's own relief at the conclusion of judgment — divine wrath is not sadistic but corrective, with a goal and a terminus.
43: ya'an asher lo zakart et-yemei neurayich: 'Because you did not remember the days of your youth.' The judicial sentence closes with the ground of the charge: forgetting (Ezekiel 16:22). The inclusion of “forgetting the days of youth” at both the beginning (Ezekiel 16:22) and end (Ezekiel 16:43) of the central indictment section creates a frame — the entire catalogue of sins is organized under the rubric of forgetting YHWH's grace. Covenant memory (anamnesis) is the foundation of covenant loyalty; its absence produces the cascade of substitutes.
44: hineh kol-hamoshel alayich yimshol lamar ka'imah bitta: 'Everyone who uses proverbs will use this proverb about you: Like mother, like daughter.' Mashal (proverb, parable) here in the sense of a popular saying. The ka'imah bitta (like mother, like daughter) proverb is applied to Jerusalem: her Hittite-Amorite origins (Ezekiel 16:3) are now visible in her behavior. The genealogical charge of Ezekiel 16:3 finds its proof in the conduct — she has become what her Canaanite parentage was, despite YHWH's transformative grace.
45: bat-imech at: 'You are your mother's daughter.' The extended application: her mother (Hittite) loathed husband and children, and her sisters (Samaria and Sodom) loathed their husbands and children. The family analogy extends the indictment to include the northern kingdom (Samaria/Aholah in Ezekiel 23) and Sodom as Jerusalem's “sisters” — all sharing the same pattern of received grace and covenant-breaking apostasy.
46: akhotach hagdolah Shomron: 'Your older sister is Samaria.' Samaria (the northern kingdom, which fell to Assyria in 722 BCE) is the older sister, Sodom the younger (pre-dating Israel's settlement). Jerusalem is placed between them, making the comparison devastating: you were meant to be better than both, but you proved worse than either. The geographic location (“to the left/north” = Samaria, “to the right/south” = Sodom) maps the covenant family onto the land.
47: velo bederkheihen halachte: 'But you did not even walk in their ways.' The comparative logic reaches its climax: Jerusalem's sins surpassed those of Samaria and Sodom. This is a prophetic scandal — Sodom is the OT's paradigm of wickedness (Gen 18-19), and to say Jerusalem exceeded Sodom is the greatest possible moral indictment. The excess is the central point: receiving greater grace demands greater response; greater grace spurned produces greater guilt.
48: chai ani ne'um adonai YHWH: 'As I live, declares the Lord YHWH.' The divine oath formula (chai ani) introduces the comparative analysis that follows. That YHWH swears by his own life (there being nothing higher to swear by, Hebrews 6:13) heightens the solemnity. The oath certifies what follows as not accusatory rhetoric but divine factual determination: Sodom and Samaria have not done what Jerusalem has done.
49: hineh zeh hayah avon Sodom achotaich: 'Behold, this was the iniquity of Sodom your sister.' Strikingly, Ezekiel's analysis of Sodom's sin focuses on social and economic sins: pride (gaon), fullness of food (sova lechem), prosperous ease (shelem hashqet), and failure to aid the poor and needy. The sexual offense of Gen 19 is subsumed under the broader category of covenantal unfaithfulness — exploitation and indifference to the vulnerable. Jerusalem's greater sin is that she had received the covenant, the temple, and the full revelation of YHWH's will, yet surpassed Sodom's record.
50: vatagevahnah vataa'seinah toevah lefanai: 'They were haughty and did abomination before me.' Gaavah (pride, haughtiness) is the ultimate root sin in the prophets — the elevation of the self above proper creaturely dependence. The toevah before YHWH likely refers to sexual offenses (Genesis 19:5; Leviticus 18:22) but Ezekiel keeps the focus on pride as the root. When I saw [it] I removed them. The removal (va'asir otam) is YHWH's sovereign act — the destruction of Sodom is placed here as YHWH's direct judicial action.
51: veShomeron kachatzeit chatotaich lo chatah: 'And Samaria — she has not committed half your sins.' The quantitative comparison is meant to shock: Samaria's idolatry, which brought Assyrian destruction in 722 BCE and which Amos, Hosea, and Micah had excoriated, was less than half of Jerusalem's sins. Vatatzdiqi et-achotaich (you have made your sisters appear righteous) — Jerusalem's excess retroactively vindicates, by comparison, the sisters whose sins were considered paradigmatic. The prophetic reductio ad absurdum exposes the depth of Jerusalem's fall.
52: gam-at se'i kelimmatach asher pilla't laachotaich: 'Also, bear your shame, you who have made judgment favorable for your sisters.' Kelimmah (shame, disgrace, ignominy) is the covenant consequence of exposed unfaithfulness. The command to bear shame is not merely punitive but formative: shame is the emotional recognition that one's behavior has violated one's identity and obligations. Jerusalem's shame is deepened by the comparative analysis — she is shamed even by comparison with Sodom.
53: veshavti et-shevutehen et-shevut Sodom vebnoteiha: 'I will restore the fortunes of Sodom and her daughters.' The sudden turn to restoration (shavah et-shevut = restore the fortunes, a standard restoration formula) is startling: even Sodom's fortunes will be restored. This is eschatological inclusion — the most notorious pagan city of the OT tradition is not beyond YHWH's restorative reach. The inclusion of Sodom, Samaria, and Jerusalem in the restoration equally honors the universal scope of covenant grace while placing Jerusalem among sinners who have no claim except YHWH's unilateral mercy.
54: lema'an tisi et-kelimmatach: 'So that you may bear your shame.' The purpose clause connects the restoration of Sodom and Samaria to Jerusalem's shame: when even Sodom is restored, Jerusalem's position as the greatest sinner among them will be permanently visible. Shame here is not destructive but formative — the proper response to grace received and spurned, which itself becomes the context in which grace is re-received.
55: veSodom achotaich uvnoteiha tashovna lekadmatan: 'Sodom and her daughters shall return to their former state.' Kadmatan (former state, what they were before) signals a restoration to the original created order before catastrophic judgment. The triple restoration — Sodom, Samaria, Jerusalem — is the eschatological inclusivism that the prophets consistently announce: YHWH's redemptive purpose will encompass more, not fewer, than Israel's election had imagined.
56: velo hayeta Sodom achotaich leshemua befich: 'Sodom your sister was not even a byword in your mouth in the day of your pride.' In Jerusalem's days of prosperity and religious confidence, Sodom was looked down upon. Geona'tech (your pride) is the sin that prevented Jerusalem from recognizing her own condition by comparison. Pride is the mechanism of self-justification that makes one blind to one's own failures while remaining acutely aware of others'.
57: beterem tigaleh ra'atach: 'Before your own wickedness was uncovered.' The temporal clause is ironic: Jerusalem was comfortable looking down on Sodom before her own sins were exposed. The exposure (galah = uncover, reveal) is both the literal exposure of the city to its enemies and the theological exposure of the covenant violations that were always present but now publicly known through the prophet's indictment.
58: zimmtatech vetoe'avotaich at-nsatim ne'um YHWH: 'Your lewdness and your abominations, you have borne them, declares YHWH.' The forensic sentence formula closes the comparative section: Jerusalem has borne (and must bear) the consequences of her own lewdness (zimah = lewdness, scheme, wickedness) and abominations. The declaration ne'um YHWH seals the judicial verdict.
59: ki koh amar adonai YHWH ve'asiti otach ka'asher asit: 'For thus says the Lord YHWH: I will deal with you as you have done.' The principle of correspondence — judgment mirrors offense — establishes the justice of what follows. The specific offense cited is the despising of the oath (bazit alah) and breaking of the covenant (heper berit). The covenant language here shifts from the marriage metaphor back to the Sinai-covenant juridical frame: the offense is treaty-breaking, and the sentence is treaty-enforcement.
60: vezakarti ani et-beriti otach bimei neurayich vahakimoti lach berit olam: 'But I will remember my covenant with you in the days of your youth, and I will establish with you an everlasting covenant.' This is the theological heart of the chapter — the climactic reversal. Ani (I myself) is emphatic — YHWH takes the initiative unilaterally. Bezakarti (I will remember) is the covenant formula for faithful loyalty: not a mere mental act but an act of committed engagement. The berit olam (everlasting covenant) is the unconditional covenant of grace that exceeds and outlasts the Sinai covenant's conditionality. It echoes the unconditional promises to Noah (Genesis 9:16), Abraham (Genesis 17:7), and David (2 Samuel 23:5).
61: vezakart et-derachayich vehikalamt: 'Then you will remember your ways and be ashamed.' The order is crucial: YHWH's remembering of the covenant (Ezekiel 16:60) precedes and enables Jerusalem's remembering of her ways (Ezekiel 16:61). The grace comes first; the shame is its fruit, not its prerequisite. Beten otach lach livnot (when I give them to you as daughters) — the restored sisters (Samaria, Sodom) will be given to Jerusalem, but not through the Sinai covenant. The qualifying phrase velo mibritech (but not from your covenant) is theologically decisive: the restoration is not the resumption of the conditioned Sinai relationship but something new.
62: vahakimoti ani et-beriti otach veyadata ki ani YHWH: 'I will establish my covenant with you, and you will know that I am YHWH.' The covenant formula and the recognition formula are joined: the establishment of the everlasting covenant produces the knowledge of YHWH. This is the goal of all of Ezekiel's judgment oracles — yadata ki ani YHWH (you will know that I am YHWH) is the book's signature phrase. The knowledge is not merely intellectual but relational acknowledgment born from covenant experience.
63: lema'an tizkheri vevoshet lach velo yihyeh lach od pitchon peh: 'So that you may remember and be ashamed, and never open your mouth again because of your shame.' The double purpose clause — remembering and being ashamed — frames the entire restoration as grace-induced humility. Lo yihyeh lach od pitchon peh (you will never open your mouth again) in the context of your shame captures the total surrender of self-justification that grace produces. The closed mouth of shame replaces the open mouth of boasting. This is not theological shame-based paralysis but the appropriate creature-response to extravagant covenant grace: speechless gratitude.
